name: Jenn McCarron
product_description: AI-powered contract data infrastructure platform that transforms legal documents into searchable business data, enabling instant contract intelligence without manual tagging.

pain_points: CLM isn't delivering on search - lawyers can't find anything
Spent years and millions on CLM implementation with underwhelming results
Manual metadata tagging is killing productivity
Finance still can't get contract data they need
Repository is a void after signature
Six weeks to produce a clause report across 100k agreements
No visibility into contract obligations and pricing terms

content_themes: CLMs excel at pre-signature but the repository is an afterthought
Contract data infrastructure is the next frontier (Legal Ops 3.0)
Point solutions beat one-stop-shops - do one thing really well
We potentiate the CLM, we don't replace it
The wow moment: contracts transformed to data in 2 minutes vs 2 years
Buyers have been conditioned to accept mediocrity in legal tech
The future of contracts is data, not documents

voice.tone: Confident, generous teacher who shares hard-won insights. Futurist who can 'draw' concepts others can't see. Direct and real - speaks from 15+ years of actually doing the work, not pontificating. Uses vivid analogies (kerplunk, stadium of data, Daft Punk robots exploding). Willing to say bold, contrarian things backed by experience.
avoids: Corporate speak and echo chamber LinkedIn fluff

key_differentiators: Former buyer turned builder - implemented CLMs at Netflix, Cisco, etc.
Technical depth - understands vectorization, LLMs, data infrastructure
Musician/producer background gives unique perspective on audience, presentation, creativity
Built something that took 2 years at Netflix in 2 minutes with AI
sample_quotes: Manual metadata tagging is over. It's over.
We potentiate the CLM. We send its value into the enterprise.
After the kerplunk... there's not a lot of intelligence. We light that up.
Do one thing and do it really well - Orville Redenbacher
They didn't know this was a problem. They thought this was just how things were.
